data.title: VXUS: Buying International Equity Still Has Upside - Strong Buy
data.description:
The Vanguard Total International Stock ETF (VXUS) seeks to track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment return of stocks issued by companies located in developed and emerging markets, excluding the United States.

I am initiating VXUS on Seeking Alpha as a strong buy, as this ETF offers exposure to grow strongly versus a falling dollar, a relative value discount versus U.S. equity, and ex-US earnings growth that is converging with the S&P 500 on consensus estimates.

On a 12-month forward basis, the MSCI ACWI ex-USD Index trades at approx. 14.6x earnings versus 22.8x for the S&P 500. This spread is the widest it has been since the GFC. Additionally, the price to cash earnings ratio of MSCI US is 2.2x the ACWI ex-US figure, which is approx. a 50% premium above the median valuation over a long period of time.

The U.S. Dollar

The general estimate from Cambridge Associates shows that the U.S. Dollar is approx. 32% higher than its real value based on equity weight. I believe that after some recent strengthening of the dollar due to the recent geopolitical conflict, I believe that the dollar will resume its weakening trend throughout this year as situations normalize.

Fidelity's research further shows that the DXY was down by approx. 9% for 2025, within December 2025, and the current tailwind had driven ex-U.S. equities to approx. 30% USD returns in 2025, while the S&P 500 returned 17.9%. Due to the lack of FX hedging in VXUS, all basis points of dollar weakness can flow directly to U.S. base holders as a gain due to foreign currency fluctuations. This is the reason I favor VXUS over a nonhedged alternative, as the outlook on the dollar, in my opinion, will fuel additional growth due to currency fluctuations on top of ex-U.S. equities growth.

Earnings

In recent years, the bear case against international equity involved ex-U.S. companies being cheaper, as they deserved to be cheaper. U.S. EPS growth had consistently outrun ex-U.S. EPS growth. This assumption is now weakening, as Schwab's 2026 international outlook shows that ex-U.S. earnings are expected to grow into double-digit rates after struggling in a flat range within 2025, with EUR and JPY financials industries leading the pack. Additionally, AllianceBernstein notes in their equity outlook that emerging markets, as well as developed markets, outpaced the S&P500 in 2025, even when denominating to local currency. This assists in the buy thesis for VXUS, as it is not just a FX thesis, but also an equities growth story as well. Between JPY corporate governance reform, Euro bank net interest margin expansion, and Emerging Markets technology buildouts, this results in a appealing blend of international developed and emerging market growth that will compliment VXUS nicely, on top of the way FX dynamics will affect international equity.

Concentration

The S&P500 has approx. 40% of the index's weight within the top 10 companies at the end of 2025. VXUS is a nice way to diversify from this exposure, as it holds more than 8,000 companies with no single company over 1% of the fund's holdings. For someone already exposed to the cap-weighted S&P, or even someone exposed to the equal-weight variant of the S&P, VXUS is an amazing single name diversifier against mega-caps, or the overall S&P, underperforming.

Inside of VXUS

VXUS tracks the FTSE Global All Cap ex US Index, which as of October 2024 contained approximately 8,408 stocks across 47 markets. Regional exposure contains approximate numbers of 38% across Europe, 27.6% in emerging markets ~25.4% in Pacific developed, 7.9% in Canada, and 1.2% in Middle East/Other. Top country exposures discussed in Vanguard's prospectus are Japan (15%), UK (9%), China (8%), Canada (7%), India (6%), France (6%), Taiwan (6%), and Switzerland (6%). Sector weights within the fund are cyclical, with Financials at 21.86%, Industrials at 15.64%, Technology at 15.30%, Consumer Cyclical at 9.05%, Basic Materials at 8.28%, and Healthcare at 7.23%.

Some additional positives to note is that VXUS holds small caps in market cap weight, which adds exposure to a size premium that has historically paid off during dollarweakening regimes. Also, the 28% exposure to EM is almost double the foreign largeblend category average. If the dollar does continue its broader weakening trend, EM equity is a high beta way to play that thesis. Also, with an expense of 5 bps, this ETF offers very cheap exposure to international developed / EM equity in a very well diversified portfolio.

Tradability

The Average daily volume of VXUS runs at approx. 7.5m shares, with $132.84B in AUM for the ETF. The bid-ask spread is also tight, between 1 and 2 cents. Premium and discount to NAV runs pretty much flat, as the fund's scale and deep participant pool by being managed within Vanguard. Additionally, the options chain is liquid, allowing for hedging strategies to be deployed (such as protective puts).

The net inflows are a positive sign too. Net inflows of $29.6B over the trailing year, and $10.8B over the trailing three months positively show that position allocators are actively moving into the ex-US trade.

Downside Risk

There is a fair downside thesis to this play as well. If the dollar reverses (whether through prolonged conflict, or the fed being forced to hold rates for longer, or raise rates as PCE reaccelerates, the dollar weakening thesis flips, which complicates Ex-U.S. equity exposure. The current base case for myself is still one rate cut in 2026, with the first cut later this year (in about September or October). I would downgrade VXUS to a hold if the fed announces that it is done entirely for the market cycle. Additionally, if there are EPS disappointments across EUR or JPY, the valuation discount would be reiterated, versus discounted, especially if the lofty double-digit EPS expectations are missed. Lastly, while less talked about recently, conflict flares between Taiwan and China (given the 7% CNY exposure and 6% to TWD) would affect VXUS largely, as 13% of the portfolio across these two would be affected.

Takeaway

The thesis to buy VXUS is clean in my view, giving the convergence between growing ex-U.S. equity alongside a weakening dollar. With the dollar being 32% above its real value, equities growth abroad will be juiced by a weakening dollar over time, making this well diversified, well positioned ex U.S. equity ETF a very attractive, strong, buy. VXUS is a great opportunity to hedge against concentration risk sitting inside the cap-weighted S&P 500. Between valuations, FX, earnings, and diversification, VXUS earns a strong buy from me.

data.title: Wendy's: Why The Turnaround Story Still Doesn't Add Up
data.description:
Introduction

I rated shares of Wendy's (WEN) as a 'sell' back in December last year following the company's Q3 results, as I felt that the company's steps to turn around the business would be challenging given the company's leverage, high deferred capex, and weak competitive positioning in the market. Given the strain on the company's financial position with high leverage, the setup has weak legs to stand on. With management announcing in Q4 that they will continue closing several stores and shift the brand to have more 'value' options for cost-conscious consumers, I'm skeptical of their ability to revitalize the business. With there still being uncertainty even at this depressed valuation, I'm maintaining my 'sell' rating, noting that there are still several challenges that lie ahead for Wendy's.

A look at Q4'25 results

Looking at the latest Q4'25 results for Wendy's, the business delivered revenues of $543 million, which was 5.5% lower compared to last year's results, as the business saw a decline in franchise royalty revenue stemming from an 11.3% drop in U.S. same-restaurant sales. Lower marketing spend has been largely correlated with a reduction in customer traffic. As well, the fact that last year had more promotions also didn't help comps in 2025. Some of those factors in the prior year included the SpongeBob collaboration and shifting the new chicken sandwich launch into 2026. On revenues, sales still came in $6.3 million ahead of sell-side estimates, which were already depressed to begin with.

The only bright spot in the top line for Wendy's has been their international business. At less than 10% of total revenues, it's an immaterial part of the business, but it's still worth noting that system-wide sales here rose 6.2%, and Q4 marked 21 consecutive quarters of growth, a stark contrast to the U.S. side of the business. Internationally, Wendy's has been growing with 59 new restaurant openings in Q4, with market entries into Armenia and Scotland and continued growth in markets like Canada, Mexico, and the Philippines.

For the full year, revenues dropped 3.1% to $2.177 billion, with global system-wide sales down 3.5% overall but international sales higher by 8.1% on 159 new openings, net unit growth exceeding 9%, and expansion into seven new markets that increased the international footprint from 31 to 38 countries. U.S. company-operated restaurants notably outperformed the broader system by 310 bps in same-restaurant sales for the year (widening to 410 basis points in Q4). That's been helped by operational improvements, better delivery growth, and digital advancements, while U.S. digital sales grew 12.4% annually, lifting the digital mix to a record 20.0% for the year and 20.6% in the quarter.

On profitability, this has been an area of weakness for Wendy's, with tough comps compared to last year. For the business, we can see that across all categories like food and paper, restaurant labor, and occupancy and advertising expenses were all higher as a percentage of revenue for 2025. Said another way, Wendy's profit margins are being squeezed as costs increase faster than revenue growth.

Wendy's still has big problems

With weak U.S. traffic and higher costs, Wendy's is facing pressures on both sides of the P&L. With company-operated restaurant margins at 12.1% in Q4 and the U.S. margin at 12.7%, both have declined significantly. In the case of the U.S. business, that was a 380 bps contraction from Q4'24 and was driven by traffic declines, commodity inflation, and labor rate increases. Even though the company had a higher average check and some labor efficiencies, this wasn't enough.

Q2'25 EBITDA was $113.3 million, down $24.2 million from last year. And EPS has dropped 36% to 16 cents. While that figure was 2 cents above sell-side estimates, generating 88 cents of earnings for what used to be a $23 stock two years ago just shows how materially the business has worsened. Management touted improvements in customer satisfaction scores for accuracy, friendliness, taste, and overall experience, but there are bigger issues here plaguing the company.

One of the big ones, I think, is capital allocation. In 2025, Wendy's paid its shareholders $330 million in dividends and buybacks while the stock. With LTM EBITDA of nearly $600 million against a debt position of $2.76 billion, I think a lot of that would have been better allocated to de-levering the balance sheet when you've got net leverage at 4.8x. Allocating that leverage could have gotten the company's leverage down a half turn and could have likely helped lower the cost of debt when it came time to refinance the $350 million of notes last year. To show you how debt isn't even remotely a priority for the company, the words 'leverage' and 'debt' are mentioned nowhere in the company's investor presentation.

Outlook

Wendy's took the Q4'25 conference call as an opportunity to lay out guidance for 2026, which was disappointing to say the least. On the call, interim CEO Ken Cook framed the year as a "rebuilding" phase under Project Fresh, with guidance for flat global systemwide sales (offset by a 4% drag from closing 5-6% of underperforming U.S. restaurants), EBITDA of $460-480 million (down due to $15-20 million from optimizations and higher G&A at ~$295 million from reset incentives), EPS of $0.56-0.60, and capex at $120-130 million with free cash flow of $190-205 million.

Overall, I generally agree that the aggressive store closures and shift to permanent value deals in tiers ($4/$6/$8 tiers) is the right move longer term. Consumers are looking for value today and are more cost-conscious than ever. There's also no reason to keep underperforming stores if value can no longer be extracted from them. In the meantime, we've seen U.S. same-store sales plunge 11.3% in Q4 amid traffic drops, signaling the need for footprint rationalization and less reliance on fleeting promotions.

That said, I disagree that U.S. same-store improvements will materialize easily, given beef/labor inflation at ~4% each and restaurant margins targeted at just 13%, which seems tight without proven traffic inflection.

Risks to Wendy's strategy

While international remains a genuine bright spot, it's frankly too small to offset U.S. drags, which make up under 10% of the system. Project Fresh's pillars (brand revitalization via quality focus on burgers/chicken tenders, ops excellence like people activation and digital menu boards hitting 20.6% mix, and disciplined capex) seem logical in theory. But in practice, I think execution risks are big given a franchise-heavy model where company stores outperformed the system by a fair margin last year.

On capex, I think Wendy's has been underinvesting in its stores, and the $122 million of planned capex investments in 2025 (shown below) won't be enough to turn the business around. The reason for this is that when we look at the company's financials in the past five years, depreciation has been running ahead of capex by almost 2x in some years. I don't think much thought has also been given to how Wendy's will support franchise owners who aren't able to invest more in the business themselves.

In the case of Restaurant Brands (QSR:CA), it's taking years to turn around the business, and the management has at least been very methodical on how they will support and incentivize franchise owners at Burger King. Some of these have included elevating the advertising fund contribution, providing cash incentives, and new equipment to simplify the job for crew members, directly lowering the labor intensity of the business for the owners, among several other initiatives. A good turnaround takes time, and good management teams understand this. But with Wendy's extracting cash from the business to fund buybacks and an unsustainable dividend, I'm not confident that they'd fall into what I'd consider to be good management for the long term for the business.

Finally, Wendy's is pushing for a turnaround in a macro environment that's incredibly tough. U.S. fast food faces headwinds from price fatigue ($15-20 combos), shrinking portions, and traffic shifts to home cooking amid cost-of-living pressures are compounding pressures. In my view, it's in these environments that you want to own the best operators in the industry. And as I'll discuss shortly, the valuation gap between Wendy's and its superior peers isn't all that wide.

With consumer sentiment soft, diverting spend from QSR despite modest restaurant industry growth, Wendy's U.S. traffic erosion can continue. Contracts like build-to-suit leases add rigidity, and elevated debt limits flexibility if the turnaround falters.

Going forward, I think growth beyond 2026 looks muted at best. Long-term goals from the 2025 Investor Day (3-4% net units, 5-6% sales growth) now ring hollow post-2025 trough. With flat 2026 sales and EPS well below prior sell-side expectations, I see a prolonged stall for the business, and so the valuation derating is completely justified in my opinion. So while the international side business can continue adding to its store count, the bulk of the business in the U.S. likely faces several pressures.

Wendy's is likely a value trap

On valuation, Wendy's has seen a sharp derating over the last few years. Since its peak valuation of 17.1x in early 2021 to 9.5x EV/EBITDA today, the company's valuation has been cut in half, with the stock falling by almost two-thirds in value (source: Bloomberg). While one may argue that the valuation likely prices in most of the risks, I think it's prudent to deduct capex from EBITDA in the denominator to get a better sense of the cash flow to the firm. That pushes the EV/(EBITDA - capex) multiple to 12.7x, which isn't cheap for a business with several structural problems and a turnaround that will take time. Moreover, as shown below, the valuation differential between Wendy's at 9.5x EV/EBITDA and the peer group at 13.6x EV/EBITDA isn't wide when you consider many of its peers are growing in the mid-single digits organically (source: Bloomberg).

Final thoughts

I'm not against investing in turnarounds. For example, in the case of Goodyear Tire (GT), investors have a business with a management team that's divested non-core assets and focused on its higher-margin brands while simultaneously reducing leverage. That helps accrue value to equity holders. So even as the stock has moved sideways, the valuation has gotten cheaper. I wrote about Goodyear last month here.

Recognizing it's a totally different business, the issue I see with the turnaround at Wendy's is that this has been a business that's been in and out of strategic review before. Historically, U.S. businesses have struggled to generate projected unit growth, and breakfast goals have not materialized despite numerous attempts. As well, the company has undergone multiple strategic reviews in the past, divesting many of its core (Tim Hortons, Arby's) and non-core businesses (Baja Fresh) to allow a more focused Wendy's brand. The more recent 2022-23 review included returning cash to shareholders over reinvesting into the U.S. system, which again looks to be the case with the current 'Project Fresh'. My worry is that this will push Wendy's further down the rankings of U.S. restaurants and lead to more issues with traffic growth.

While it's tempting to argue that the company would be better off as a private company, high current leverage limits interest from a financial buyer, and increasing capital requirements for existing units would likely keep buyers away. With a shrinking business and no permanent CEO since July 2025, I'm still skeptical about the business long term. For these reasons, I maintain my 'sell' rating on the stock.

data.title: The Hormuz Ledger: U.S.-Iran Crypto Convergence
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Insider info: The White House warns staff against placing bets on Polymarket and Kalshi.

Historic mission: Artemis II crew heads back to earth, with a splashdown expected tonight.

Calls for a truce: Russia and Ukraine have agreed to a ceasefire - but only temporarily.

Tolls and tokens

The current shipping situation in the Strait of Hormuz is pretty cryptic and is also getting more crypto-esque by the day. The critical waterway was supposed to be fully open to traffic by now, but according to scans of the area, only several tankers have transited the chokepoint. Compounding the problems is that Iran wants to start charging pass-through tolls, which cannot be sanctioned or touched by the Western financial system.

Quote: "Every very large crude carrier (VLCC) transiting the strait has been paying $2M (or $1 per barrel of oil), in line with what has been under discussion in parliament," said Hamid Hosseini, a spokesperson for Iran's oil, gas, and petrochemical products exporters' union. "Once the email arrives and Iran completes its assessment, vessels are given a few seconds to pay in bitcoin, ensuring they can't be traced or confiscated due to sanctions."

It's a similar playbook taken by Russia in recent years to upend dollar supremacy, conducting international trade and cross-border transactions in crypto and other modes of currency. Iran's crypto ecosystem even reached $7.8B in 2025, according to blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis. Payments have spanned commodities like oil and illicit arms transfers, as well as reaching regional militia proxies and paying foreigners to gather intelligence.

Interesting paradox: The embrace of alternative payment systems hasn't only been celebrated by Washington's adversaries. It goes all the way to the White House. President Trump has promised a new era for digital finance to "ensure that the United States will be the crypto capital of the planet and Bitcoin superpower of the world." A similar stance has been present surrounding tolls in Hormuz, where Trump, at times, has pitched a joint U.S.-Iranian collection system, calling it a "beautiful thing," but has also floated the idea of the U.S. alone charging tolls or promoting the "free traffic of oil." data.title: 10-14% Yields - 2 Deeply Undervalued BDCs The Market Has Completely Mispriced
data.description:
The market is currently selling off even high-performing business development companies (BDCs) (BIZD) due to concerns about AI disruption of software, potentially leading to losses on software loans. There are also broader concerns about the health of the private credit sector, and even the economy as a whole, in the wake of spiking energy prices. This is creating opportunities to buy high-yielding income machines at compelling valuations, and in today's article, I'm going to detail two that I've been buying recently.

The Gold Standard of BDCs

The first one I'm going to talk about is Ares Capital (ARCC), which is broadly considered to be the gold standard of externally managed BDCs. It is managed by Ares Management (ARES), which is a large and proven direct lending investor and manager, and ARCC itself has one of the longest and most impressive BDC track records, dating back to before the Great Financial Crisis. Over the long term, it has delivered stellar total returns for shareholders.

ARCC is well diversified across 588 companies in its portfolio, and its portfolio is well diversified in a way that gives it an all-weather-type performance profile, with 66% allocated to senior secured loans. It also has meaningful exposure to junior debt and equity investments that can give it capital appreciation and higher-yielding potential. Given its skilled underwriting, it is able to balance these portfolio trades to deliver attractive total return without suffering too much downside during economic downturns.

While 24% software exposure is likely one of the main reasons why the stock has sold off recently, it has a 40% weighted average loan-to-value along these lines and, to date, has an excellent track record. It has been underwriting AI risk for a while and is focused on names that are better positioned to weather potential AI disruption of the software space.

ARCC's Rock-Solid Portfolio Metrics

Its portfolio today also looks quite healthy, with non-accruals at just 1.8% on a cost basis. While its leverage is at 1.08x on a net debt-to-equity basis, it earns a solid investment-grade credit rating, as well as meaningful financial flexibility to its 1.25x upper target limit. If it needs to take on extra leverage to address opportunities, whether it be buying back its own stock now that it trades at a material discount to NAV or investing in loans that provide very attractive risk-adjusted return potential, either would likely boost its NII per share and further support its current dividend. It does have a $1 billion share purchase program in place but has yet to report any repurchases.

Its dividend per share currently yields about 10.7% and is covered 1.08x by NII, which is fairly solid for the BDC space and can have an extra cushion in the event that management decides to take on a bit more leverage to further juice NII per share. Given that ARCC has historically traded in line with or even at a premium to NAV, today's discount of around 10% below NAV makes it a highly compelling buy in my view.

Why Blue Owl Technology Finance Is Even More Intriguing

Another high-yielding BDC that I like a lot right now is Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF), which is, as its name indicates, managed by Blue Owl Capital (OWL), which is also a leading direct lender. In particular, it has a prestigious track record in software lending, with not a single loan default not being worked out, and actually a net gain on all of its investments over time rather than a loss ratio, which is virtually unheard of in the private credit (PBDC) industry, especially over the lengthy track record that Blue Owl has in software lending.

It is also worth noting that about 15% of OTF's portfolio is invested in equity-like investments, including a substantial stake in SpaceX that has appreciated significantly over time. Due to a merger between xAI and SpaceX, Q1 is likely to experience a significant markup in its upcoming quarterly report as well, with further upside catalysts and liquidity catalysts later this year via SpaceX's IPO, if that were to happen. It would not only likely further boost OTF's share price but also give it proceeds that it could reinvest in income-generating assets and/or buy back its own stock at a deep discount, which would be further NAV accretive and NII per share accretive.

The Two Big Risks Facing OTF Today

The two big risks facing OTF today are:

  • Its 70% exposure to software

  • Its covered dividend is only generated at 30 cents in adjusted EPS per share this past quarter, as opposed to the 35 cents per share quarterly dividend that it is paying out

However, it is important to note that this past quarter it had a 0.66x average leverage ratio, which is way below its 1.25x upper leverage target, and therefore means that its NII per share should be ramping significantly in the coming quarters as it buys back stock at a deep discount to NAV and invests in new opportunities that come its way. Additionally, software loan spreads are likely increasing given the concerns about the space and the way software stocks and software lenders have sold off so far this year, which means that their investments should be even more accretive than they would be otherwise in terms of NII per share.

A Balance Sheet Built to Go on Offense

OTF also has a $300 million buyback plan in place, after repurchasing $65 million of stock in Q4 alone, which means that it is serious about buying back stock at an over 30% discount to NAV. These buybacks will be accretive not only to NII per share but also to NAV per share. Additionally, its low leverage, with its very strong underwriting performance to the tune of just 0.4% loan accruals at cost and 0.2% non-accruals at fair value, and investment-grade credit ratings from multiple agencies, underscores the strength of its balance sheet. It has $2.3 billion in available liquidity, and the vast majority of its debt maturities are not due until 2029 or later. It is also taking steps to improve its cost of capital through CLOs and SPV activity, and its portfolio is very healthy, with 81% invested in senior secured loans across 199 portfolio companies, a weighted average loan-to-value of just 34%, only 0.5% of its investments at a risk rating of 4, and 0% at risk rating 5.

Between its 35 cents per quarter dividend and the 5 cents per quarter special dividend that is declared for this year, it currently yields over 14%, alongside its fairly aggressive stock buyback cadence and its over 30% discount to NAV.

Why These Two Discounted Income Machines Are Must-Buys Right Now

Both ARCC and OTF are exposed to software risk and general concerns about the health of private credit. However, both are trading at substantial discounts to NAV, have strong balance sheets, have very strong underwriting performance, and have sustainable dividends that yield between 10.7% and over 14%. Therefore, I view them as highly compelling buys right now and have made them two positions that I am buying at the moment, alongside several other deeply undervalued, high-yielding opportunities in my 7-8% yielding, total return outperforming, and below-market-beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: How Policymakers Can Lower Mortgage Costs And Increase Housing Affordability
data.description:
Ever since the pandemic - when surging housing demand collided with a decade of underbuilding - housing affordability has become an increasingly important political issue and a larger focus for policymakers. President Donald Trump has made it a component of his agenda heading into the midterm elections in November, including issuing several executive orders on housing (read them here), and Congress has recently taken steps to pass legislation that, among other things, would reduce the federal regulatory burden on new home construction, though much of the red tape exists at the local level.

While we believe housing affordability has improved and will continue to do so (read our recent commentary), some policy ideas circulating in Washington, DC could marginally enhance affordability, while others, albeit well-intentioned, could actually hinder affordability. We enumerate and expand on these concepts:

1) Changing the mix of agency mortgage-backed security (MBS) purchases and increasing the $200 billion amount could help lower mortgage rates. In January, President Donald Trump announced that Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac – government-sponsored entities (GSEs) – would purchase up to $200 billion of agency MBS in the coming months. The goal is to increase demand, which typically raises bond prices and lowers yields, thereby reducing mortgage rates.

Mortgage rates initially declined following the announcement – the 30-year mortgage rate fell below 6% for the first time in more than three years. However, rates have since rebounded amid the Iranian conflict as well as due to confusion about the GSE purchase program’s objectives and which mortgages the GSEs are purchasing. There has also been concern that $200 billion is too small relative to the roughly $9 trillion agency MBS market to materially impact rates.

To maximize effectiveness, we believe Fannie and Freddie should concentrate purchases in segments where rate impacts would be most significant (the so-called “par coupon”). Additionally, we think the Trump administration should consider expanding purchases beyond $200 billion – indeed, even announcing an increase could lower rates. Lastly, while the Federal Reserve should remain independent, mortgage rates could decrease further if the Fed halted the roll-off of mortgage bonds on its balance sheet (read more in our commentary "A Fed Housing Fix That’s Hiding in Plain Sight").

We believe the GSEs buying the par coupon and the Fed stopping the run-off could decrease mortgage rates up to 25 basis points (bps), with further rate declines possible if GSE MBS purchases were to increase. These policies have the added benefit of not requiring Congressional approval.

2) Clarifying the future of the GSEs could also lower mortgage rates. We have written extensively about the future of the GSEs and maintain that policymakers should “not fix what is not broken” (read more here). We believe the GSEs are doing an excellent job of delivering mortgage liquidity and providing access to mortgage credit, not to mention they are profitable (and at times, significantly so), benefiting taxpayers – a nice reversal, since taxpayers funded the bailout of the GSEs during the 2008 financial crisis.

Despite functioning well in their current state, there has been ongoing discussion and speculation about the possible public offering of Fannie and Freddie stock. We believe talk alone of a GSE IPO may be self-defeating as it raises questions about future government support. This uncertainty can add risk premium to MBS, increasing mortgage rates for borrowers. As such, if the administration were simply to stop talking about a potential IPO of the GSEs, we could see some of this uncertainty premium decrease and rates could decline by ~10bps.

Of course, Congress could eliminate this uncertainty altogether – and lower mortgage rates significantly – by passing legislation to explicitly guarantee all GSE mortgage-backed securities. Doing so would provide significant (and welcome) clarity to investors globally about the current and future state of the GSEs and likely lead to even more demand from investors. Such a change could also lead to increased appetite for MBS from banks due to better risk-weighting under the existing capital treatment rules.

3) Modifying the GSE rules to limit the number of investment properties allowable could – at the margin – increase the supply of housing. A recent executive order (read it here) requires the Treasury to evaluate the impact of purchases by institutional investors on the housing market and propose recommendations. From our perspective, these institutional investors, primarily single-family rental (SFR) companies, are not significantly exacerbating affordability (see Figure 1). Indeed, rents have declined while costs have increased, reducing the investment appeal of large-scale acquisitions. Regardless, such a policy would require Congressional action, and we do not think the policy has sufficient support in Congress as of now.

Figure 1: Acquisition by single family rental operators has declined

Still, policymakers could more effectively limit potential investor crowding-out without Congress; the GSEs could limit the number of homes they guarantee for investment purposes. Currently qualified investors can finance up to 10 properties. This policy is relatively new and was borne out of a time – in 2009 – when the U.S. had “too many homes” and policymakers decided to raise the number of allowable properties to 10. This seems silly now, and we believe an update could and should be implemented to ease supply to ensure access for first-time homebuyers.

4) If a “g-fee” cut is considered at all, it should be narrowly targeted to avoid unintended consequences. We do not believe that policymakers need to (or should) cut the “g-fee,” the fee that GSEs charge to cover the costs of guaranteeing an underlying mortgage. These fees are small relative to the overall mortgage rate and are an important component of Fannie and Freddie’s profitability and capital accumulation. Indeed, given the cloud of uncertainty in the market regarding this issue, mortgage rates could decline by five to 10 bps if policymakers were to simply announce that they will not cut the g-fee.

If policymakers pursue this idea, we believe it is essential that the g-fee reduction be limited to first-time homebuyers and not those looking to refinance. If a g-fee cut were implemented across-the-board, it would likely lead to higher, not lower mortgage rates.

Why? One of the primary reasons agency MBS trade at higher yields than U.S. Treasuries, even though they are both government-guaranteed (and bear the same credit risk), is because MBS are “callable” – borrowers can pre-pay their mortgages at any time. Investors require greater yield as compensation for the potential that borrowers will prepay and investors will get their money back before they want it. Given a broad-based g-fee cut would make bonds more callable as more borrowers would refinance, we would expect mortgage rates to increase, rather than to decrease, since MBS investors would demand a higher yield premium. Instead, if the cut were targeted only at new first-time homebuyers, a broad-based increase would not occur, which would better achieve the goal of policymakers to lower rates for home purchasers.

5) Avoid policies that could unintentionally increase mortgage rates. Similar to an across-the-board g-fee cut, we believe policymakers should avoid other policies that could increase mortgage rates because they either increase prepayment risk (investors getting their money when they don’t want it – i.e., rates are decreasing) or increase extension risk (investors not getting their money back when they do want it – rates are increasing). Both outcomes lead to investors demanding more MBS yield, driving up mortgage rates. Such policies include 1) a cut to the loan level pricing adjustments (LLPA) – one-time, upfront fees charged to lenders and passed onto borrowers – which would lead to prepayment risk, 2) funding a 50-year mortgage, which could lead to prepayment risk, as well as much less equity build-up, and 3) allowing mortgage portability – a borrower could transfer their mortgage to another property – which would lead to extension risk.

6) End homebuilder buydowns, which artificially inflate home prices. As housing affordability has declined, homebuilders have started offering “buydowns,” providing borrowers a below-market mortgage rate (typically one to two percentage points lower). While this reduces the interest homebuyers pay, homebuilders pair the lower rate with a higher priced home. This may sound good in theory, but in practice it inflates the price of the home above where it would typically clear and can artificially elevate neighboring home prices as well. Should the homebuyer need to sell shortly after purchase, the realized value would likely be well below the original sale price, exposing both taxpayers and subsidized homeowners to risk. Also, the homebuyer loses the opportunity to refinance if interest rates decline one or two points in the future – i.e., they could have bought a cheaper home at a higher mortgage rate and then refinanced if rates declined, enjoying a lower monthly payment. Finally, home insurance and property taxes are higher because of the artificially elevated home price. Overall, this practice further exacerbates affordability issues. We believe Ginnie Mae and the Federal Housing Authority (FHA) should limit this practice, something they have the power to do unilaterally.

Conclusion

We expect some improvements in housing affordability will occur organically, so it may behoove policymakers to do very little. In fact, they could help lower rates by simply clarifying they are not going to IPO the GSEs and will not pursue a broad-based g-fee cut.

If policymakers take a more active approach, however, they should ensure their policies do what they are intended to do – decrease mortgage rates – and avoid unintended consequences that accomplish the opposite.

If all the policies outlined above were pursued, we believe mortgage rates would decline by nearly 75 bps, bringing the mortgage rate closer to 5%, than the 6% we see today.

data.title: Adobe: Hated, Cheap, And Growing
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Adobe Inc. (ADBE) has fallen 32.63% over the past twelve months and is trading 66.85% below its all-time high of $688 in 2021. Three forces led the decline: a sector-wide SaaS de-rating, fears that generative AI will make Adobe less competitive, and uncertainty regarding the CEO transition. The market has punished the stock accordingly, but I think it is overdone. In the article, I discuss the reasons why I think Adobe is undervalued and warrants a Strong Buy rating.

Business Overview

Adobe operates under three segments. Digital Media (Creative Cloud and Document Cloud) accounts for 75% of revenue; Digital Experience (Adobe Experience Platform, GenStudio, Marketo, Adobe Commerce) accounts for 24%; and the legacy Publishing and Advertising segment accounts for <1%.

Nearly all of Adobe’s sales are subscription revenue. In Q1 FY2026, subscriptions totalled $6.17 billion, or 96% of the top line. Distribution runs through the main website, app stores, enterprise contracts, and resellers. Gross margins are 89%, while TTM ARR was at $26.06 billion.

Starting in Q1 FY2026, Adobe restructured its reporting around customer groups. Business Professionals and Consumers subscription revenue grew 16% YoY to $1.8 billion, while Creative and Marketing Professionals subscription revenue grew 12% YoY to $4.4 billion. Enterprise momentum was particularly notable, with AEP, native apps, and GenStudio ending ARR growing over 30% YoY. Adobe’s top-line growth has been consistent, ranging between +10% and ~13% in the past 12 quarters.

Valuation

Adobe is trading at $229.94. Its valuation and financials against peers are outlined below.

Multiples indicate that Adobe is cheap relative to peers. Adobe trades at 3.81x EV/Sales and 3.82x P/S vs. the peer average at 18.58x and 18.37x, respectively. Even the cheapest peer, ORCL at 6.21x P/S, trades at a ~63% premium to Adobe. On P/B, Adobe is 8.17x versus a peer average of 29.61x; only MSFT (6.99x) is cheaper on this metric.

Adobe’s balance sheet quality is adequate. The current ratio (0.91) is below the peer average (1.96), but net debt-to-assets is essentially zero (-0.01). Interest coverage is 37.11x, second only to MSFT (67.25x) and well above the peer average (19.82x). Capex/D&A is 0.25x, the lowest in the peer group, which reflects Adobe's asset-light model. The accruals ratio (-0.11) is negative, indicating that earnings are backed by cash flow rather than accounting adjustments. The company also repurchased 5.6% of shares outstanding over the past year.

To quantify how much of Adobe’s current market cap reflects growth expectations, using data from WRDS, I decompose the stock’s market cap into earnings power value (EPV, i.e., value assuming zero growth) and market-implied value of growth (MIVoG) below. Cost of equity is derived from a Fama-French three-factor regression.

The cost of equity is in line with sector and market averages. The discrepancy, however, is in growth. The market prices Adobe at an implied growth rate of 5.17%, below both the IT sector mean (8.49%) and the S&P 500 mean (8.28%). 5.17% is low, considering how Adobe's revenue grew 10~13% for 12 consecutive quarters. Currently, only 44.67% of Adobe’s market cap is attributable to growth, vs. 72.17% for the IT sector and 67.66% for the S&P 500.

Re-rating Adobe’s MIVoG to the IT sector mean implies $475.83, or ~106.9% upside. Re-rating to the S&P 500 mean implies $409.39, or ~78% upside. Both assume no change to the cost of equity or current earnings power.

Catalysts

I see four catalysts for Adobe.

First, competition from low-cost providers like Canva and Midjourney targets Adobe's least profitable users, not its most profitable ones. Among the strongest bear arguments is that cheaper generative AI alternatives will detract from Adobe's user base. There are two reasons why this argument is weak. First, the users low-cost platforms tend to attract are casual users (e.g., students, freelancers), who are Adobe's lowest-ARPU customers. Most of Adobe's revenue comes from professionals and enterprises. These users face much higher switching costs, since they have their entire workflows (e.g., file formats, plugins, collaboration tools, etc.) already built around the Adobe ecosystem. Second, Adobe is not ignoring the casual tier. Adobe Express crossed 80 million freemium MAUs in Q1 FY2026 (up 50% YoY) and serves as a funnel into the paid ecosystem.

Second, AI monetisation is growing and sticking. Adobe reported that AI-first ARR more than tripled YoY in Q1 FY2026. Generative credit consumption is expanding into higher-compute modalities like video and audio. Total MAUs surpassed 850 million, up 17% YoY. Creative freemium MAUs crossed 80 million, up 50%. Management also noted that AI generation credits are increasingly functioning as a workflow tokenisation mechanism rather than a novelty feature. Management expects this dynamic to accelerate in the back half of FY2026.

Third, the NVIDIA partnership upgrades the infrastructure behind Firefly. Adobe’s recent deal with NVIDIA will allow Firefly models to be trained on NVIDIA's specialised AI hardware and software (CUDA for GPU programming, NeMo for model training, and Cosmos for pre-built open-source AI models). Firefly outputs should get meaningfully better and faster as a result.

In addition to improving model quality, Adobe will also use NVIDIA's Agent Toolkit to build autonomous AI workflows: AI systems that execute multi-step marketing production tasks (content generation, resizing, localisation, distribution) with minimal human input. The partnership includes a cloud-native 3D digital twin solution on NVIDIA Omniverse as well, allowing brands to create photorealistic virtual product replicas for marketing without physical photoshoots.

Adobe’s deal with NVIDIA will likely widen the quality gap between Firefly and competitors who lack access to NVIDIA's compute stack, making it progressively harder for startups or open-source alternatives to match Adobe at enterprise scale.

Fourth, Adobe’s Semrush acquisition extends reach in the GEO market. Adobe agreed to buy Semrush in a $1.9 billion all-cash deal, which has been approved by Semrush shareholders and Germany's Bundeskartellamt. This is important because consumer discovery is shifting from traditional search to AI-generated answers (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini). If a brand does not appear in those AI answers, it is invisible. Semrush is the leading platform building tools for this new category, known as generative engine optimisation (GEO), and its platform draws on 26.5 billion keywords and 43 trillion backlinks. By integrating Semrush, Adobe can offer a full closed loop, where users can create content in Creative Cloud, design campaigns in Experience Platform, and track and optimise their content in both traditional and AI search. No competitor offers this.

The deal is expected to close in Q2 FY2026. Adobe is paying approximately 3.7~4.5x Semrush's annual revenue (~$428M-$445M). For a high-growth SaaS company with 33% enterprise ARR growth, that is reasonable. For context, the failed Figma bid in 2023 was priced at approximately 50x revenue. Semrush's stock had fallen approximately 40% before the offer. Adobe was bought at a favourable entry point.

Risks

There are two risks to my thesis.

First, the CEO transition introduces uncertainty in execution. Narayen led Adobe for 18 years, during which he oversaw the company’s transformation from selling perpetual licenses to the creative cloud giant it is today. In March 2026, he announced that he will step down once a successor is named—the board has formed a special committee to evaluate candidates, though no successor nor timeline has been disclosed yet.

Leadership transitions can be risky because they can lead to shifts in strategy or the departure of talent. Markets could apply a higher discount rate until a new leadership is confirmed. However, it is unlikely that deals mentioned previously (the NVIDIA partnership and Semrush purchase) are to be affected, as they are contractually already in motion.

Second, competitive intensity in creative tools is accelerating. Companies like Google, Canva, and other AI-native startups are increasingly threatening Adobe’s core creative markets by making it cheaper and easier to make images and videos. This threat is real and could become more serious in the future. However, for now, I find this concern overstated because Adobe’s moat is not Photoshop or any single software in isolation, but is the integrated workflow that spans the whole creative process. Adobe also has a lot more cash than most firms to outspend challengers on R&D or to acquire smaller competitors once their products have proven market fit.

Conclusion

Adobe is cheap. It trades at 3.81x EV/sales vs. a peer average of 18.58x. The MIVoG analysis prices Adobe at 5.17% implied long-run growth, below both the IT sector mean (8.49%) and the S&P 500 mean (8.28%), despite revenue growing at 12%. Catalysts are clear—AI monetisation success, NVIDIA partnership, strategic Semrush acquisition, and overdone fears vis-a-vis low-cost competitors. In my view, these reasons are sufficient to rate the stock a Strong Buy. However, I would reconsider my rating if subscription revenue growth falls below 10% for two consecutive quarters or if significant management issues arise during the succession process.

data.title: Marching Through Iran - A First Quarter 2026 Review
data.description:
The month of March followed the old aphorism nicely from a weather and climate perspective. For most of the country, it went in like a lion with harsh weather conditions, while the month ended like a lamb with relatively mild weather. The stock market, however, was another story altogether. It came in with hopeful trepidation like a rabbit and exited like a rabid dog, biting and chewing anything its size that entered its path. No one’s quite sure whether the “mad dog” is clearing the way for a bull, a bear, or something unknown after the first week of April that consisted mostly of buying the dip in US markets.

Most investors are well aware that in the long term, the markets always shrug off even the most major geopolitical disturbances. But some are worried that this may be the exception that proves the rule. At any rate, there can be little doubt that the aggressive war in the name of the preemptive strike that the US and Israel are waging in Iran has injected a level of uncertainty into the markets that hasn’t been seen in most of our lifetimes. Without going into details, the high stakes, potential perils, and complete unknowns are exigent factors that are difficult if not impossible to integrate into even the most sophisticated of automated trading models – even those using AI. At ValuEngine, we will concede that the longer such an environment envelops the world, the more difficult it becomes to use risk and predictive models, and it will be difficult to distinguish leading future performers from laggards. However, ValuEngine’s models usually do very well when major geopolitical issues begin to wind down and markets regain some semblance of normalcy.

Fortunately, hindsight is still 20/20, so one thing we can have confidence in is our annual first-quarter performance review. There is an interesting breakpoint here. The first two months saw momentum increase slightly on one of the trades that started in July 2025 and permeated the rest of the year. That was easing out of positions in “the Magnificent Seven” mega-cap tech giants and other large-cap growth stocks and into previously underperforming small-cap and value stocks. The month of March did not discriminate that much and took all major US equity market size and style ETFs down at least 3.8% and at most 6.4%. This is a relatively sharp decline for just one month.

Interestingly, although IWN (the iShares Russell 2000 Value ETF) had the best or least bad month by “only losing” 3.8%, its counterpart IWO, representing small-cap growth, lost 6.4%. This is the worst of any of the size and style index ETFs. Between skyrocketing fuel costs and an expected credit crunch as a result of a perfect geopolitical storm, small-cap stocks took a real beating in the second half of the month.

For the entire quarter, the prior two-month trend prevailed. Large-cap growth stocks were used by many as sources of funds for buying smaller-cap stocks and stocks that are more value-oriented. As a result, IWN had the best quarterly price change, up 4.9%, while the Vanguard US Growth ETF, VUG, lost 10.4%, making it by far the worst performer. QQQ, an Invesco ETF representing the Nasdaq 100, was the second-worst quarterly performer with a 5.9% loss. The second-best performer was VTV, the Vanguard Large Cap ETF.

Energy is clearly the big story here. The State Street Select Sector SPDR Energy, XLE, had already come back from being dead last in 12-month performance among the Select Sector SPDR ETFs for the period ending March 31, 2025, to third in overall 12-month performance when March began. By March 31, 2026, it had captured the top spot by a wide margin with a whopping 34.7% return as compared with the 29.4% posted by the prior runaway leader, State Street Select Sector SPDR Technology, XLK. Altogether, XLE gained 37.9% in the 1st quarter, including 10.3% in March. On the other hand, the second-best performing Select Sector SPDR for the quarter, XLB, containing the materials and mining stocks in the S&P 500, slid back 6% in the same month which cut its 1st quarter gain to 10.7%.

Two sector ETFs that have been stagnant for the better part of a year are XLV which owns Health Care stocks, and XLF representing the Finance Sector. Since both are currently rated 2, ValuEngine’s predictive model is not expecting a rebound from either in the near future.

For individual stocks in the first quarter of 2026, energy and technology infrastructure companies dominated the top performers list of U.S. mega-cap stocks. SanDisk (SNDK) led the group with a surge of over 130%, followed by significant gains in the energy sector as oil prices reacted to geopolitical tensions. SNDK benefited from a global NAND flash shortage and the massive expansion of AI data centers. Exxon Mobil (XOM), ConocoPhillips (COP), and Chevron (CVX) all posted record quarterly gains courtesy of global political tensions. GE Vernova (GEV) saw significant gains as investors rotated into “defensive growth” companies supporting the global energy transition and grid infrastructure needs. The following table summarizes the quarter from the perspective of five stocks. All are rated buy or strong buy by ValuEngine with the exception of Chevron.

The top five stocks previously mentioned (SanDisk and energy majors) massively outperformed the seven traditional tech titans, which all finished the quarter in negative territory. Interestingly, the two with the smallest losses are rated as strong buy stocks by ValuEngine, while the other five are rated “hold,” and this hierarchy of order held before the quarter began.

Is the market buying the dip during the first week in April a sign that the market is ready to return to normal? That is highly doubtful.

What is not doubtful is that investors are most often poorly served by letting global tensions affect their investment decisions. This is why quantitatively driven services such as ValuEngine exist. We provide a consistent baseline against which other investing ideas can be benchmarked. ValuEngine’s models historically have performed best when major geopolitical and other events begin to wind down and ‘data’ begins to mean something again. The recovery is often fast and furious.

data.title: Bitmine Immersion: Market Is Missing The Hybrid Ethereum Model
data.description:
Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR) is being increasingly misunderstood as just another crypto treasury vehicle, which overlooks the changes taking place in the business. While other crypto treasuries rely on underlying asset appreciation, BMNR is working toward a fundamentally different approach. This involves the same large-scale acquisition of Ethereum, but combined with income-generating infrastructure and institutional-grade access to the Ethereum network. In my opinion, the full potential of BMNR's strategy and differentiation isn't fully priced in. With crypto prices down over 50% from all-time highs and an mNAV below 1x, the risk/reward is compelling for a treasury such as BMNR.

While the market saw plenty of passive treasury models appear throughout 2025, BMNR made it clear from the beginning that this would not be a passive treasury model. Capital raising was specifically linked to purchasing ETH and funding staking, validation, and overall blockchain-related financial activity. The more important trend, however, may be the increasing role that scale is playing. Scale is not only a result of success in crypto equity markets; it is a competitive advantage. Small treasury vehicles may attract short-term retail investment; however, they rarely develop into institutional trading tools. By comparison, BMNR's sizeable balance sheet and trading liquidity likely make it a top choice for Ethereum exposure.

Simultaneously, BMNR is adding layers of infrastructure via initiatives such as its validator network, becoming more active in the Ethereum ecosystem. This creates a wider range of value drivers than just the appreciation in ETH prices, with options to benefit from staking yields and services. This turns BMNR into a hybrid model of part treasury and part infrastructure platform, which aligns the company more closely with how institutional investors use these assets.

Overall, a publicly traded vehicle such as BMNR that offers scale, transparency, liquidity, and yield will be a beneficiary as Ethereum continues to serve as the primary settlement and tokenization layer for the blockchain. This is something that has a chance to gain premium pricing over time as these values become fully priced in. At a time when BMNR is trading below the value of its assets and crypto prices are down, it makes an attractive entry point for long-term investors looking to bet on the future of tokenization and institutional crypto adoption.

Scale Has Become a Moat

In the initial 8-K filing, BMNR stated that they were raising capital for the express purpose of acquiring ETH. The company also indicated that they will utilize at least 20% of net proceeds to fund income-producing blockchain-related projects, and 5% of net proceeds to fund validator, wrapping, and staking technology. This differentiates the goal from other passive treasuries in the space.

Since the most recent update, BMNR now owns 4% of the global ETH supply with over 4.8 million tokens. The company also holds $864 million in cash, $92 million of Eightco Holdings (ORBS), and $200 million of Beast Industries. With cash to opportunistically buy ETH at current low levels, no debt, and some diversified investments, BMNR's balance sheet is in good shape.

This size matters for two primary reasons. First, size provides relevance and allows them to be adopted by institutions, not just retail investors. Secondly, once a company grows as much as BMNR did and becomes the largest publicly traded ETH treasury, it keeps the company on the radar of investors, whereas other treasuries have lost all their hype. This, combined with blockchain initiatives, is why BMNR appears far more interesting than other smaller treasury stock peers. By remaining the largest and most involved in the Ethereum ecosystem, I believe BMNR has developed a moat through its ability to establish itself as a highly liquid name before anyone else.

Why Ethereum Is the Right Choice

Since Ethereum can be productive, it looks like a stronger choice to build around rather than Bitcoin (BTC). Treasury companies for Bitcoin are almost completely dependent on the BTC price and the appetite for treasuries within the marketplace. BMNR has both of these same variables when it comes to ETH, but it also has staking and a much more defined connection to building out blockchain-based financial infrastructures.

There are already examples of this "productivity" coming into play in how management is structuring the business. Bitmine announced in its last update that there were approximately 3.3 million ETH being staked for over $7.1 billion. Additionally, management launched their Made in America Validator Network (MAVAN), which is described as an institutional grade staking platform and will be used not only to support the Bitmine treasury but will ultimately be available to institutional players and other companies in the ecosystem. BMNR says the rewards from MAVAN will be around $300 million annually, which is real revenue currently not priced into the stock, considering it trades below its NAV. That's just from their holdings, something that has the potential to multiply as institutional players look to enter the ecosystem.

This allows the company to potentially see upside from three different directions if things continue as planned. This includes an increase in ETH prices, a greater number of ETH being staked as they continue to grow holdings, and increased value from the services surrounding MAVAN. These developments and revenue streams don't currently appear to be factored into the valuation, but should allow BMNR to claim a premium above its NAV as results materialize.

Institutional Adoption and Legislation

A number of regulatory hurdles have been removed to allow institutional investment, and there are more on the way. With a pro-crypto Administration and the March 2026 SEC-CFTC Landmark Memorandum of Understanding, there have been strong strides taken towards allowing institutions more access in the space. The 2026 EY Parthenon Institutional Digital Asset Survey shows a great example of the potential, with 73% of institutional investors indicating they will be increasing their allocation of crypto by the end of the year. In the same study, the largest risk is regulatory uncertainty, something that is being treated by regulations in the pipeline. However, it can be mitigated by investing in a public entity like BMNR.

Regulation is the clear direction institutional investors are looking for, which is clearly shown in EY’s results of the survey. The study also shows the importance of governance, high levels of liquidity, and risk management, all of which are incorporated in BMNR. It may also be preferential to some institutional investors to not deal with the operational aspects like taking possession of assets, managing wallets, staking, or transacting directly on-chain. Instead, many might prefer to gain access to cryptocurrency in public markets that meet their existing requirements and procedures.

In the end, a move to crypto benefits BMNR either way. With more regulatory clarity and expectations of increased positions, ETH will likely receive benefits from more involvement in the crypto space, regardless of if investors choose BMNR as their preferred vehicle. Regulations like the CLARITY Act are working their way through the Senate to create a definition for digital commodities, which would define Ethereum under CFTC regulation and remove the "security" label previously assigned to the crypto as a result of prior court decisions. Additionally, the repeal of SAB 121 will enable major institutional firms to hold crypto on their books. The SEC's March 2026 guidance also makes it clear that protocol-level staking is not considered a securities offering, which makes the MAVAN business model available for institutions. EY reported an increased focus on platform readiness, which means as Wall Street develops its tokenization framework using public blockchains, Ethereum is likely to be the largest beneficiary of that migration. Since BMNR serves as a vehicle for institutional investors who seek exposure to Ethereum and staking yields without any of the technical or regulatory headaches that go along with it, it is positioned to benefit the most. This could either be through direct investment or investment in the underlying ETH asset. With these tailwinds in crypto, especially ETH with its tokenization/staking capabilities, BMNR looks primed for a comeback in 2026.

BMNR Remains Relevant

Although the initial movement in the stock price of BMNR was influenced largely by hype, the long-term vision was always to be an investment vehicle. Despite falling ETH prices, BMNR announced that its average daily trading volume (ADTV) is approximately $987 million, which ranks it the 96th most traded stock in the US. Besides liquidity giving it prestige, it also represents a technical requirement for institutional investors who want to purchase equities without too much slippage.

In addition to the volume at which BMNR is currently trading, the company is also relocating from the NYSE American tier to the main NYSE board, which could allow access to institutions that restrict purchases of equities listed on secondary exchange tiers.

Valuation

The major misconception with BMNR is that the market views it as a passively operated treasury vehicle and values it strictly against its holdings. However, properly evaluating the hybrid model of this company as a whole requires combining all the elements of its business. BMNR's valuation floor at a 1x mNAV is about $11.4 billion (when adding up cash and ETH assets at a price of $2,200). Therefore, buying BMNR's stock at levels below this, which it's currently trading under, gives shareholders value from their assets alone, without accounting for additional revenue streams.

The most undervalued aspect of BMNR is that it has developed the MAVAN infrastructure, which, at current rates, generates around $300 million annually in staking rewards using BMNR's own treasury. Utilizing a 10x revenue multiple for MAVAN at current revenue expectations of $300 million provides a valuation of MAVAN at $3 billion, before accounting for the potential upside of having MAVAN open to third-party institutions or ETH prices rising. This makes the value of BMNR $14.4 billion currently, without any ETH appreciation. This is an upside of around 32% from BMNR's current market cap of around $10.9 billion, and I believe this is fair given MAVAN's potential and the current discount in treasury value. Besides immediate upside, I see ETH appreciation as likely from crypto and tokenization tailwinds as well as upside from MAVAN with institutional use. Due to these factors, I'm rating BMNR a Strong Buy.

Risks to the Thesis

Dilution is a concern. If the cash raised is used in a way not aligned with investors' expectations, it could hurt BMNR's valuation.

Crypto volatility is a risk, with prices well off recent highs. Since BMNR is tied to ETH, it could push its valuation lower if there is any weakness.

If regulations are delayed or unfavorable regulations are added, it could hurt the thesis. Given the current pro-crypto administration and support for regulatory bills, I see this as unlikely.

Takeaway

BMNR has differentiated itself from traditional cryptocurrency treasury vehicles by combining large-scale ownership of Ethereum with an income-generating infrastructure. The company's scale, ownership of ETH, strong liquidity, and trading volume position it as a preferred vehicle for institutional investors seeking direct exposure to Ethereum.

Besides tailwinds, BMNR's current business isn't fully priced into the valuation. The marketplace still views BMNR too much like a passive treasury even though there are signs of additional revenue streams and upside from infrastructure development. Since the stock trades below the mNAV of BMNR, investors are essentially getting BMNR's staking platform and growth potential as an institutionally recognized vehicle at a discount. As regulatory clarity improves and institutional allocations to digital assets increase, BMNR will likely benefit from the company's direct growth and ETH's price appreciation. With crypto prices down and regulations in the pipeline, BMNR looks attractive at its current discounted valuation, which is why I'm rating the company a Strong Buy.

data.title: Humana Is Still A Hold Despite The Medicare Advantage Rate Boost
data.description:
Humana’s (HUM) situation isn’t smooth right now. It’s a big name in the industry, but near-term profits are slipping, people aren’t exactly feeling great about it, and the stock isn’t obviously cheap, so you don’t get much of a bargain even if things bounce back. Health insurance stocks surged after the Trump administration announced that average Medicare Advantage payments will increase by 2.48%, in 2027. But that bump doesn’t change the basic story. I can’t say the stock deserves a higher multiple, or that there’s some clear upside here, unless the company shows real progress on three things: fixing margins after getting hit on Star Ratings, picking up the pace on new member growth, and actually cutting its underlying costs. Until those start to show up in the numbers, I’m sticking with a Hold. There’s always a chance things turn fast, but as it stands, the stock isn’t dirt cheap, just maybe a bit undervalued at best.

Looking back to the last time I checked in, not much has really sorted itself out. The stock dropped from $260.28 to $197.32 over that stretch.

Medicare Dependence

Humana’s insurance business still basically lives or dies by what happens with its Medicare Advantage plan. In 2025, total Medicare accounted for 82.6% of consolidated premiums and services revenue, while total insurance premiums revenue accounted for 95.5%. Premiums came in 9.6% higher, up to $122.8 billion for 2025. That’s mostly because per-member Medicare premiums went up, thanks to a bigger direct subsidy from the IRA and higher per-member state-based contract premiums and membership growth. But on the flip side, individual MA membership dropped 7.3% to 5.25 million after Humana hiked prices and pulled out of markets that weren’t making money. Total Medicare membership shrank 2.5% to 8.28 million. State-based membership did jump, though, up 10.7% to 1.62 million. Standalone PDP membership climbed 7.6%. CenterWell, which handles care delivery, saw total external services revenue rise 39%, with pharmacy solutions up 34.7% and primary care up 76.0%. Still, higher costs (risk model tweaks, changes in patient mix) wiped out most of those gains, so operating profits ended up almost flat compared to last year at $1.3 billion.

Margins And Cash

For 2025, the benefit ratio came in at 90.4%. $1.0 billion in favorable development helped prevent margins from slipping even more. The operating cost ratio edged down to 9.1%. Net income for Humana was $1.2 billion, or $9.84 a share, weighed down by tighter margins and several items, including value-creation charges, impairment charges, other expenses, and a loss on the sale of business. On a reported basis, diluted EPS was $9.84 versus adjusted EPS of $17.14, a difference of $7.30. Net cash provided by operating activities was $921 million, which keeps liquidity solid even with lower earnings. The balance sheet looks steady: $4.2 billion of cash and cash equivalents and $12.4 billion of long-term debt, and a $0.885 quarterly dividend that’s sustainable, but not exactly generous. Capital spending stays pretty disciplined, but there’s not much wiggle room unless profits start to pick up.

Valuation

Humana sits at about 22 times expected earnings for 2026 and about 13 times 2027 consensus earnings. Cigna, for comparison, trades at only 9.2 times 2026 earnings, and Elevance at 12.2 times. Those two also show better returns on equity and better operating margins. Humana’s dividend yield is 1.8%. That’s less than Cigna and Elevance at ~2.2%. Price-to-book looks similar, but Humana’s return on equity, just over 7%, lags well behind Cigna (15.1%) and Elevance (13.2%). At $197.32 now, I don’t see enough room for error here to call it a buy unless something new and positive shows up.

Rate Relief

The Medicare Advantage rate hike was a real boost for short-term earnings expectations. The whole sector jumped as investors stopped playing it safe. “Insurers to receive extra $18B as Medicare overhauls Star Ratings system”—that's a solid win. The same with news about Humana telling people they expect 25% MA membership growth in 2026. But the actual benefit from better Star Ratings and rate changes needs some context. Membership has dropped, and bonuses tied to ratings are still way down. Management is trying to save costs by improving tech and their processes, but they also pointed out their risk from the court loss about Star Ratings. As they say, “We have filed a lawsuit seeking to set aside and vacate the 2025 Star Ratings of our Medicare Advantage plans, but there is no assurance that we will prevail...” (HUM, 10-K) What this means for 2026 and after, if Star Ratings don’t rebound, bonus payments stay low, which squeezes both growth and margins. Over time, that’s a big problem.

Upside Triggers

Two things could push the stock up. If Humana actually pulls off the 25% Medicare Advantage growth they’re shooting for in 2026, or if court cases and rule changes bring the higher Star Ratings back, then the built-in margin and profit potential could look better by 2027. Saving through multiyear efficiency work around outsourcing, automation, and supplier consolidation isn’t huge for a company this size, but it would help, especially if they can improve net margin from its current under-1% and don’t get hit by more ratings or payment surprises. Management said they are pursuing multiyear cost-efficiency programs and transformation work, so if revenue grows but fixed costs stay put, profits could get a boost.

Risks: Stay Clear

If they can’t get back to a 4-star rating and keep it, earnings could stay under pressure through 2026 and 2027. If cash keeps flowing out, they might have to sell assets or cut the dividend. Unexpected hits to margins, changes to government payments, and higher costs in the Medicare Advantage market all make things tougher. The industry is shifting, there aren’t big hurdles for new competitors, and price is what most customers care about. Management said, “If our credit ratings were to be lowered, our cost of borrowing likely would increase, our sales and earnings could decrease, and our results of operations, financial position, and cash flows may be materially adversely affected.” (HUM, 10-K) If better-rated competitors keep taking business, Humana’s lower margins could stick unless something changes.

What Changes It

The next year or two will settle things. In the short run, keep an eye on what happens with the lawsuits about Star Ratings and how quickly new rules show up in bonus payments. The 2026 Medicare Advantage bid and whether they hit their membership growth target (+25%) will be proof of whether things are actually turning. If those numbers don’t come back, Humana will stay stuck, investors waiting for real proof. If the company starts showing better results, more signups, lower costs, or improved ratings, I’d consider changing my view to buy. That would mean earnings can actually get back to normal, and maybe the stock price catches up. But if they lose control of expenses or ratings don’t move, there’s more room to fall, and earnings could get knocked down again.

Hold For Now

Right now, I’m still holding. It’s not about whether Humana’s got problems; everyone knows that. The real question is what actually has to change, in the numbers and how the business runs, before it’s worth getting optimistic. I can’t see enough progress yet: not just a lift from higher Medicare rates, but real improvement in profits and where Humana stands against the competition. What would make me flip to a more positive call? Actual, reported progress, better Star Ratings, more members, lower costs, stronger margins.

data.title: Interactive Brokers: A Compounding Machine That Deserves To Be Bought Patiently
data.description:
My Investment Thesis

Interactive Brokers (IBKR) is, in my opinion, one of the highest-quality platforms in global electronic brokerage. It has an unusually scalable, automation-first operating model and a revenue mix that benefits when client assets and interest-bearing accounts expand. The fundamentals have been strong. In 2025, net revenues were $6.205 billion, with income before taxes of $4.771 billion (a ~77% pre-tax margin) and net income available to common stockholders of $984 million (approx. $2.22 diluted EPS).

Recent developments worth noting:

  • The company announced expanded crypto portfolio transfer capabilities (a small but directionally important piece of product completeness for multi-asset “all-in-one” brokerage)
  • Also, IBKR’s inclusion in the S&P 500 (effective August 28, 2025, per index announcement) likely improved liquidity and structurally increased passive ownership demand

My valuation analysis leads me to a fair value that is below the current share price; an uptrend in price is still compelling if growth and rates cooperate, but the bear scenario could become real if rates fall and/or markets de-risk. In conclusion, even though the company can seem overvalued, my rating is a Hold, given the high quality of its business and competitive advantages. I currently hold IBKR in my portfolio, and I’ll wait for further drops in price to increase my position.

Company Snapshot

Interactive Brokers is not a typical consumer brokerage. In my opinion, it is better understood as a scaled financial infrastructure platform wrapped in a broker interface. IBKR operates in more than 170 market centers, supports 29 currencies, and serves clients in more than 40 countries.

The company specializes in routing orders and executing and processing trades in basically all available asset classes and derivative products (e.g., stocks, bonds, commodities, options, futures, etc.) for different types of clients (hedge and mutual funds, ETFs, advisors, brokers, individual investors, etc.).

IBKR's platforms offer global access for a wide variety of client trading needs and experience levels:

IBKR has experienced tremendous growth in popularity over the last 5-10 years, as highlighted by the double-digit growth in both client accounts and client equity:

Business Model

Explained simply, IBKR makes money in two main ways. First, it earns net interest income on client cash, margin loans, securities lending-related balances, and segregated cash. Second, it earns transaction-based revenue (aka commissions) when clients trade stocks, options, futures, forex, bonds, and other instruments. Smaller lines include fees, services, and miscellaneous other income. The company’s growth strategy is to keep lowering friction for clients, widen the platform’s use cases, and let scale do the rest.

In practical terms, management’s playbook has been remarkably consistent for years: build software once, add more markets and products, keep operating costs low, pass much of the cost advantage to clients, and then grow accounts, client assets, and margin balances. That is why I think IBKR is better described as a compounding platform than a traditional broker.
