Historical Growth and Profitability

Over the last five fiscal years (2021-2025), IBKR’s growth and profitability have been exceptional, as reported:

  • Net revenues CAGR ~18%
  • Income before taxes CAGR ~22%
  • Net Income CAGR ~26%
  • Pre-tax margins (income before tax / net revenues) were roughly ~65% in 2021 and ~77% in 2025
  • Net income available for common stockholders as a share of net revenues increased from roughly ~11% in 2021 to ~16% in 2025

What stands out to me is not just “high margins” but improving efficiency: non-interest expenses were $1.434B in 2025 versus $1.490B in 2024, even as revenue rose. This supports the idea that the model is operationally scalable.

Moat and Competitive Landscape

In my opinion, IBKR’s moat is not brand in the consumer sense; it’s process and infrastructure.

The following is my qualitative take, not a quantified claim:

Cost advantage/operating efficiency: 9/10. The sustained high pre-tax margins and improving expense ratio strongly suggest a structural cost advantage rooted in automation.

Switching costs: 8/10. Once a client has multi-asset positions, tax lots, margin setups, integrations (APIs), and operational workflows, switching is painful—especially for active/pro users. The firm’s broad market access and tools deepen that stickiness.

Pricing power: 6/10. IBKR is known for competitive pricing; pricing power shows up less through raising commissions and more through (a) spreads/interest economics and (b) value-based retention driven by execution and tools.

Brand strength: 6/10. Among professionals and serious retail traders, IBKR is a strong brand; among casual retail, less so.

Network effects: 4/10. This isn’t a social network. There are some indirect network effects (liquidity access, relationships with venues, scale economics), but it’s not a classic winner-take-all network model.

IBKR competes with a range of retail and institutional brokers. In the U.S. retail ecosystem, the scale leaders by client assets are far larger. For example, at year-end 2025:

  • The Charles Schwab Corporation reported $11.90 trillion in client assets
  • Robinhood Markets, Inc. reported $324.2 billion of total platform assets
  • LPL Financial Holdings Inc. reported $2.4 trillion of total advisory and brokerage assets

IBKR’s reported customer equity was $779.9 billion on the 31st of December 2025, which puts it below the very largest custody platforms but still firmly in “major scaled player” territory—especially considering its global multi-asset positioning and professional-trader skew.

My Understanding of Management, Strategy, and Outlook

I think management quality is high. Thomas Peterffy’s (founder and chairman) long record still shapes the culture, and the operating DNA is visible in the numbers: very high margins, tight expense control, relentless platform improvement, and no appetite for flashy empire-building.

Topic

Assessment

Capital allocation

ROE averaged roughly 20% over the last five years; investment is focused on platform/product rather than acquisitions

Buybacks

Not a major part of the story; share count has gradually risen (~5.6% CAGR over the last 10 years) with compensation and structure-related exchanges

Dividends

Modest (~0.5% div yield) but rising; 2025 dividend per share was $0.30, and quarterly cash dividend reached $0.08 in early 2026

M&A history

Limited; this is largely an internally built compounding story

Insider alignment

High directors and executive officers’ control (~3%)

Executive pay

Meaningful stock-based compensation (~62% of the total) for operating executives; overall alignment looks reasonable

I tried to synthesize my understanding of IBKR’s strategy in the following four points:

1. Build best-in-class automation and risk controls: enables low marginal cost per additional account and per incremental trade

2. Compete aggressively on price/value (commissions, margin rates, interest paid depending on program/eligibility): attracts active and professional users

3. Expand globally and broaden product access (many exchanges, many currencies, Visa card, AI-powered trading tools, new features like crypto transfers, coins, and stablecoins): increases the addressable market and wallet share

4. Monetize growing customer equity and balances primarily through net interest income and other balance-dependent economics

The firm’s revenue mix has shifted toward net interest income in the higher-rate era, while customer scale and DARTs (daily average revenue trades) have still grown strongly.

As for what concerns the company's outlook, I would not consider 2021–2025 growth rates mechanically because part of the increase was related to the interest-rate regime. That said, I think IBKR can plausibly compound earnings at an above-market rate for a while if:

  • Account growth stays healthy
  • Customer equity trends upward with markets
  • Net interest economics remain supportive (even with lower rates, volumes and balances can offset)

But if we enter a multi-year lower-rate environment with weaker risk appetite, IBKR’s net interest tailwind can fade, and earnings growth could normalize drastically.

Industry size and growth are difficult to estimate precisely because “brokerage” can be defined narrowly (retail online brokerage) or broadly (securities brokerage & capital markets services). Public market research estimates for online trading platforms often show mid-to-high single-digit or low-double-digit CAGRs (~5%) into the late 2020s/early 2030s.

My takeaway is not the exact dollar figure; it’s the industry trend: more self-directed investing globally, more cross-border product demand, and more expectations of institutional-grade execution and risk controls from a retail-facing interface.

Possible Challenges and Headwinds

Interest rate risk (biggest macro lever): IBKR explicitly discloses sensitivity; for example, a 0.25% decrease in U.S. dollar interest rates is shown as reducing annualized net interest income by ~$77 million (per its interest sensitivity discussion). Even if balances grow, a fast rate decline compresses spread economics and can reduce earnings power.

Market activity risk (trading volumes are cyclical): The company states plainly that revenues depend on trading volume and interest rate levels and can fluctuate significantly. In a low-volatility bull environment, trading can soften; in a crisis, activity can surge, but credit/default risk can rise.

Margin/default and counterparty risk: As a broker extending margin loans and operating in derivatives markets, IBKR is exposed to client default risk and counterparty exposures—though the model emphasizes real-time margining and automated liquidation to mitigate loss.

Regulatory and compliance risk: This is a heavily regulated business across many jurisdictions. IBKR discloses broad regulatory and compliance risks (including international regulatory complexity), and real-world enforcement exists: the Office of Foreign Assets Control (under the U.S. Department of the Treasury) announced a settlement with Interactive Brokers LLC related to apparent sanctions violations, with a settlement amount of ~$11.8 million (July 15, 2025).

Technology and cybersecurity risk: The firm’s reliance on technology and third-party systems is a known vulnerability, and risk factors explicitly discuss cybersecurity incidents and third‑party system reliance as potential material risks.

The stock can be considered overvalued, but possible entry points are not far away

A classic free-cash-flow DCF is not clean for broker-dealers (customer cash movements distort operating cash flow), so I preferred to base my valuation analysis on the company’s multiples and their comparison with peers/industry and historical values. Finally, the technical analysis will allow us to further evaluate the current valuation of the company.

As shown in the table below, IBKR presents higher valuation multiples compared to its industry peers:

A further look into historical values shows how the main multiples considered (LTM P/BVS, NTM P/E, NTM P/S) present higher figures compared not only to the lows of the last 10/15 years but also to their mean values.

At today’s market price (~$67 as of 7th of April), the stock trades at approximately 31x trailing earnings, which is not “cheap” for a broker, even a very good one like IBKR.

Now let’s integrate some technical analysis into the decision-making process to assess the stock’s price action.

Looking at the weekly chart for the last 3/4 years, we can clearly see the big run-up IBKR’s stock price has experienced in the last two and a half years. From the local low of ~$19 in November 2023, the stock surged ~303%, reaching a local peak of ~$78 in January 2026.

The stock recently tested the weekly 50 SMA (yellow circle), which has worked as a strong support for the price in the past. If the price drops further, the first target could be represented by the support zone in the area of $58-60 (blue line), which has been tested in the past. This could mean a decline of ~10-11% from the current price.

Should the price drop even more, a second target could be represented by the trend line (white), which has already offered good support in 2024 and 2025, as shown in the graph below. This could mean a decline of ~25-30% from the current price to reach the area of $47-50.

Takeaway

If I didn’t own IBKR, I would not be excited to initiate a full position at ~31x TTM earnings unless I had a strong view that earnings growth will remain unusually high and the market will keep paying a premium multiple. If I did own it (and this is my case), I’d be reluctant to sell a high-quality compounder outright, but I would personally prefer to add on meaningful pullbacks.

The long-term story (global scale, automation, product breadth, interest-driven engine) is compelling, but today’s valuation leaves less room for disappointment, especially if rates or market activity move the wrong way. These are the reasons that explain my Hold rating.
